data.title: Phillips 66 ordered to pay $800M penalty in California biofuel trade secrets case
data.description:
Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) was ordered to pay $800M in damages to biofuel maker Propel Fuels for stealing trade secrets to build up its renewable fuel capabilities, Reuters reported Thursday, citing a California state court document.

The court in Alameda, California, told Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) to pay $195M in punitive damages, in addition to $604.9M in compensation the refiner was ordered to pay following a separate verdict.

"The court finds that Phillips 66's misconduct was 'reprehensible' from a business perspective," the court order said. "The evidence at trial reflects that Phillips 66 took advantage of Propel Fuels by abusing its bargaining power during due diligence."

In October 2024, a jury determined that Phillips 66 (PSX) stole trade secrets under the guise of gathering information for a potential acquisition and then used the information to create a competing business.

data.title: TreeHouse Foods reiterates $345M–$375M adjusted EBITDA guidance and targets $250M supply chain savings through 2027 while closing two plants
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TreeHouse Foods, Inc. (THS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Steven T. Oakland, President, CEO & Chairman, reported, "We achieved adjusted net sales and adjusted EBITDA results that exceeded the upper end of our guidance ranges. This performance further demonstrates the execution of our margin improvement plan we discussed with you earlier this year." Oakland noted the company’s focus on controlling costs and executing plans to drive profits and cash flow amid macro headwinds. He emphasized ongoing efforts to reduce structural costs and align the business with near-term realities, highlighting, "We are focused on execution, maintaining our improved service levels and our griddle business is now in a place to positively impact our results in the second half of the year." Oakland also announced the decision to close two plants in the pickles and cookies businesses to “rightsize our network,” stating, "We believe these strategic decisions improve our competitive positioning and also allow us to be more flexible with capital, focusing our investments in areas that will provide better margin profiles and growth potential."

  • Patrick M. O’Donnell, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Our adjusted net sales were up 1.4% year-over-year. We delivered strong adjusted EBITDA of $73.3 million, which was up about 4% year-over-year. Our adjusted EBITDA margin rose 20 basis points to 9.1%." O’Donnell outlined that the acquisition of Harris Tea added almost 5% to sales, while commodity-related pricing, primarily within coffee, contributed approximately 4%.

Outlook

  • O’Donnell reaffirmed, "We are anticipating our full year adjusted net sales will grow between negative 0.5% and 1% year-over-year or in a range of $3.36 billion to $3.415 billion." Company volume and mix is expected to decline about 1% year-over-year. Harris Tea’s contribution is expected to be offset by the ready-to-drink business exit, margin management actions, and the griddle recovery. O’Donnell reiterated, "We are reiterating our adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $345 million to $375 million. We are also reiterating our free cash flow expectations of at least $130 million." Third quarter adjusted net sales are anticipated in the range of $840 million to $870 million, with adjusted EBITDA expected to be between $90 million and $110 million.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net sales rose 1.4% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA totaled $73.3 million, up about 4% year-over-year. The adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 20 basis points to 9.1%. O’Donnell explained, "Volume and mix provided a drag of $1.1 million, driven by lower volumes, including the impact of our margin management actions, which were largely offset by Harris Tea volume." Pricing net of commodities (PNOC) was a $9.7 million drag due to commodity inflation, while operations and supply chain improvements contributed $10.6 million in benefits. O’Donnell also described cost reduction efforts, which delivered a $2.9 million benefit in SG&A and other expenses.

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: Asked about narrowing price gaps between branded and private label, and competitive dynamics. Oakland responded, "We've actually moved our prices the same or maybe even $0.01 or $0.02 less when it comes to shelf price," and noted the company performed at or above private label share in most top categories, attributing the remaining variance to customer mix.

  • James Ronald Salera, Stephens Inc.: Inquired about innovation and its role in private label’s positioning. Oakland explained, "The nice thing about private label from an innovation standpoint is we are fast followers... We have it in coffee with cold brew... in pretzels with seasoned and filled."

  • Robert Bain Moskow, TD Cowen: Asked about the coffee segment and the impact of tariffs. Oakland stated, "We see demand for -- and we see opportunity in ground coffee right now... That work is just finishing as we speak right now. So it's giving us capability. We're literally bidding on projects that we could have never bid on before."

  • Matthew Edward Smith, Stifel: Queried about volume trends and margin management. O’Donnell noted, "We're embedding, I would say, more of the same," and Oakland added, "We see '26 as the start of a growth year, not another margin management year."

  • Scott Michael Marks, Jefferies: Requested clarity on Q3 volume/mix guidance. O’Donnell clarified, "From a Q3 standpoint, we'll have a couple of things coming towards us. I think we're expecting consumer trend to be more or less the same."

  • John Joseph Baumgartner, Mizuho: Asked about promotional intensity and gross margin evolution. Oakland said, "The message we're getting from the retailer... suggests that they're going to support private label as well." O’Donnell indicated, "I think you'd see it sort of flattish through the third quarter, and then you'll start to see it improve over the fourth quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns around pricing gaps, category performance, innovation, and the impact of tariffs, displaying a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they probed for clarity on volume trends, cost structure changes, and margin management.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing cost control, margin management, and future growth opportunities. In Q&A, Oakland and O’Donnell provided detailed responses and reiterated confidence, with Oakland stating, "We think the year will fold out just about as we expected."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone remained neutral with persistent focus on volume and margin trends, while management’s confidence appeared steady, supported by repeated reference to execution and strategic discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow was reiterated quarter-over-quarter, with a slight increase in full-year adjusted net sales expectations. The company advanced from restoring its griddle facility in Q1 to asserting it will positively impact results in the second half. Strategic plant closures in Q2, compared to management streamlining in Q1, signal more aggressive cost structure optimization. Management’s tone remains confident, while analysts’ focus persists on volume, innovation, and margin trajectory.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing commodity inflation, promotional intensity, and macroeconomic headwinds as challenges. The impact of tariffs, especially on coffee, was noted, with mitigation strategies including alternative sourcing and pricing. Plant closures were executed to optimize the production network and reduce costs. Analysts raised concerns about volume declines, pricing actions, and potential gross margin pressure, especially in the context of promotional activity and commodity costs.

Final Takeaway

TreeHouse Foods emphasized disciplined execution on its margin improvement plan, with adjusted EBITDA and net sales surpassing guidance ranges. The company reiterated its full-year financial outlook, highlighted a continued focus on profitability and cash flow, and announced strategic plant closures to optimize its network. Management remains confident in its ability to navigate headwinds, control costs, and position the business for growth, particularly as the benefits of margin management and supply chain initiatives are realized through 2025 and into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chevron to deliver oil to Venezuela under new production terms - Bloomberg
data.description:
Chevron (NYSE:CVX) will hand over a percentage of its Venezuelan production to the Maduro government under new terms negotiated after receiving a U.S. license to restart operations, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The deal reportedly was signed between Chevron (NYSE:CVX) and Petroleos de Venezuela in recent days after the U.S. company received its new private license, under terms similar to those reached by other foreign energy companies operating in Venezuela, which also deliver a percentage of their production to the Venezuelan government as payment.

The Trump administration sought to make certain that Chevron (CVX) paid no royalties or taxes in cash to the Maduro government, but obtaining oil instead of cash effectively means the regime has another way to get revenue, by selling its portion of the production to other buyers, although it is not yet clear if Venezuela will be allowed to export the oil to the U.S. or other markets.

Trump's recent pivot on Venezuela, restoring Chevron's (CVX) production, means the country will be able to sustain oil production of ~900K bbl/day and potentially increase it to 1M bbl/day late next year, while otherwise output was seen falling to as low as 600K bbl/day, according to Rapidan Energy.

Since the expiration of the previous licenses issued by the U.S. Treasury Department at the end of May, China had become virtually the only buyer of Venezuelan crude.

data.title: Aurinia raises 2025 revenue outlook to $270M as LUPKYNIS accelerates market penetration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Aurinia Pharmaceuticals (AUPH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Peter S. Greenleaf, President and CEO, reported "strong growth in total revenue and net product sales in the 3 and 6 months ended June 30, 2025," attributing the increase primarily to higher LUPKYNIS sales and deeper market penetration in lupus nephritis.
  • Greenleaf announced the company is "increasing our full year 2025 total revenue guidance from a range of $250 million to $260 million to a range of $260 million to $270 million and our net product sales guidance from a range of $240 million to $250 million to a range of $250 million to $260 million." The company also highlighted positive results from its aritinercept Phase I study and indicated further clinical studies will launch in the second half of the year.
  • Joseph M. Miller, CFO, stated, "We are continuing to be opportunistic with our share repurchase plan and expect to fund any future discretionary share repurchases with cash flows from operations and cash currently on hand. The company repurchased 18.3 million of its common shares for $138.4 million, excluding commissions and excise tax since the launch of the program in the first quarter of 2024 through today."

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year 2025 total revenue guidance to $260 million–$270 million and net product sales guidance to $250 million–$260 million, citing sustained growth in LUPKYNIS sales and market expansion, particularly in rheumatology and hospital segments.
  • Greenleaf explained that the guidance range accounts for historical patterns, stating the lower end assumes "flattening of our business or a declining of our business in the summer months and back to growth in the back half of the year," while the upper end reflects "continued growth through 3Q and 4Q."
  • Further clinical studies for aritinercept in at least two autoimmune diseases are planned for the second half of 2025, with no additional specifics disclosed for competitive reasons.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the three and six months ended June 30, 2025, was $70 million and $132.5 million, up from $57.2 million and $107.5 million in the comparable periods of 2024.
  • Net product sales of LUPKYNIS reached $66.6 million for the quarter and $126.5 million for six months, compared to $55 million and $103.1 million in the same periods last year.
  • Cash flow generated from operations for the first half was $45.5 million, versus negative $2.8 million used in operations a year earlier; excluding $11.5 million in restructuring payments, operational cash flow was $57 million.
  • Gross margin improved to 90% for the quarter (88% for six months), compared to 84% and 85% in 2024, with total operating expenses dropping to $49.9 million for the quarter and $90.5 million for six months, reflecting cost controls and restructuring.
  • Net income for the quarter was $21.5 million, or $0.16 per share, and $44.9 million, or $0.33 per share for the six months, compared to $722,000 and a net loss of $10 million, respectively, in the prior year.

Q&A

  • Vishwesh Shah, TD Cowen: Asked about the value and development trajectory of aritinercept and the impact of ACR guidelines on LUPKYNIS adoption. Greenleaf responded that the company sees "upwards of 20 to north of 20 different B-cell-mediated diseases" as potential targets for aritinercept, but specifics are withheld for competitive reasons. Keenan confirmed a Q4-week dosing schedule for aritinercept. Greenleaf noted "a really strong uptick in the number of rheumatology prescribers" and growth in hospital business following updated guidelines.
  • James Alexander Stewart Vane-Tempest, Jefferies: Inquired about drivers behind guidance and LUPKYNIS new patient/hospital trends. Greenleaf outlined that "the best way to think about our guidance range is on the lower end to the midpoint...you'd have to see either a flattening of our business or a declining of our business in the summer months and back to growth in the back half of the year."
  • Will Devroe Soghikian, Leerink Partners: Asked about formulation work for AUR200 and expectations for R&D spend. Greenleaf said development is "parallel pathing" for patient-friendly formulations, possibly including an auto-injector. Miller stated, "We haven't provided any specific guidance on operating expenses and/or cash flows going forward."
  • Yu He, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about presenting SAD data and disclosure timing for aritinercept indications. Keenan stated data would be presented at an upcoming meeting, not yet determined. Greenleaf indicated disclosure will occur "when we move into a certain phase of development," as required by public filings.
  • Douglas Miehm, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about Paragraph IV filers and buyback strategy. Greenleaf confirmed "no change...in terms of the exclusivity period" and reiterated the company's "unique position as a biotech company in this space to pay our bills" while funding both R&D and share repurchases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a positive to slightly positive tone, congratulating management on the quarter and focusing on growth drivers and pipeline progress. They expressed keen interest in guidance drivers, product adoption, and next steps for the aritinercept program.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, especially in prepared remarks, with Greenleaf frequently referencing the company's "continued growth," "encouragement" from new guidelines, and operational efficiency. During Q&A, responses were consistent and occasionally cautious regarding competitive disclosure, using phrases like "we're not disclosing for competitive purposes."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited slightly more optimism, with more focus on positive guidance revisions and less concern about cost structure and legal risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised full-year revenue and product sales guidance in Q2 2025, whereas Q1 guidance was unchanged.
  • Management's tone in Q2 was more assertive, highlighting operational cash flow strength, expanded share buybacks, and successful restructuring outcomes.
  • Analysts in Q2 were more focused on drivers of growth, adoption in new prescriber segments, and the pipeline's competitive positioning, compared to Q1's emphasis on cost management and legal risks.
  • Key metric changes quarter-over-quarter include increased revenue, higher net product sales, improved gross margin, and a stronger cash flow from operations.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward leveraging new ACR guidelines and accelerating aritinercept's clinical development.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing patent litigation related to Paragraph IV filers but emphasized a strong IP position with patents extending to 2037.
  • The company acknowledged increasing R&D costs as aritinercept advances, but Miller reiterated a commitment to operational efficiency and cash flow discipline.
  • Analysts inquired about the potential impact of increased R&D spend on future share repurchases, to which management cited flexibility given current cash flow and balance sheet strength.
  • Disclosure on the specifics of aritinercept's future indications is intentionally limited to maintain competitive advantage.

Final Takeaway

Aurinia Pharmaceuticals delivered a quarter marked by rising LUPKYNIS sales, improved financial metrics, and an upward revision to 2025 revenue and product sales guidance. Management credited market penetration and guideline adoption for the robust performance and highlighted the progress of aritinercept, though further details remain under wraps for competitive reasons. Operational efficiency, strong cash generation, and an expanded share repurchase program remain central to the company’s strategy as it advances its commercial and clinical priorities for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold demand rose in Q2 on continued ETF inflows, central bank purchases - WGC
data.description:
Gold demand rose during Q2, helped by persistent inflows into gold ETFs, the World Gold Council said Thursday in a new report on gold demand trends.

Total Q2 gold demand rose 3% Y/Y to 1,249 metric tons, as ETF inflows totaled 170 tons, compared to outflows of 7.1 tons in the year-earlier quarter, driven by uncertain global trade policy, geopolitical turbulence and rising gold prices, although the increase was much slower than exceptionally strong Q1 inflows of 226.6 tons.

Central bank demand for gold slowed in Q2 to 166 tons of purchases, the lowest quarter since 2022 and down from 249 tons in Q1, but demand remains significantly higher than typical quarterly levels during 2010-21.

Central banks are strategic, long-term buyers, but they are not completely insensitive toward gold prices, so gold's YTD rally likely has contributed to the slowdown, although the fact that banks still continue to add gold underscores generally favorable attitudes, the WGC report said.

Gold futures climbed to an all-time record high of $3,509.90/oz on April 22, and Nymex gold futures are up more than 25% YTD.

On Thursday, gold futures traded sideways as markets digested the Federal Reserve's decision to hold interest rates steady and the flurry of tariff announcements ahead of the August 1 deadline to reach trade deals or suffer higher U.S. tariff rates.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for August delivery closed -0.1% to $3,293.20/oz, and front-month August Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished -2.7% to $36.552/oz, the sixth loss in the past seven sessions for both metals; for the month of July, gold came in flat while silver rose 1.9%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (NYSEARCA:IAU), (NYSEARCA:NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Ag Growth International reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Ag Growth International  press release (TSX:AFN:CA): Q2 Revenue of $349M.
    • Adjusted EBITDA1 of $54 million, towards the high-end of the $50-$55 million outlook provided by AGI
    • Adjusted EBITDA margin %2 of 15.6% was impacted by a segment mix with a higher weighting of Commercial segment revenue relative to Farm segment revenue
    • Free cash flow1 of $0.3 million on a last twelve months (“LTM”) basis ending June 30, 2025, mostly due to temporary working capital requirements related to large projects in international Commercial
    • Net debt leverage ratio2 of 3.9x at June 30, 2025 vs 3.6x at March 31, 2025 and 3.1x at June 30, 2024
    • An approximate $9 million reduction in professional fees associated with the strategic review process conducted in 2024

    Outlook

    • Adjusted EBITDA guidance for the full year 2025 remains consistent with expectations for at least $225 million1
    • Commercial segment visibility for the second half of 2025 is strong, supported by a healthy order book
    • Farm segment visibility to the second half of 2025 remains limited due to challenging market conditions
    • Based on current tariff policies and regulations, we estimate a relatively minor direct cost impact to AGI in 2025, and it has been factored into our outlook
    • Order book3 up 4% YOY to $660 million as of June 30, 2025, supported by significant growth within our international Commercial businesses
    • After the quarter, significant international Commercial momentum continued with several notable order commitments secured across a mix of geographies with an aggregate value exceeding $100 million
    •

data.title: Cullen/Frost outlines 6%–7% net interest income growth for 2025 as branch expansion accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cullen/Frost Bankers, Inc. (CFR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Phillip D. Green, Chairman and CEO, reported that Cullen/Frost earned $155.3 million or $2.39 per share in Q2 2025, compared with $143.8 million or $2.21 per share in the same quarter last year. Green highlighted, "Average deposits in the second quarter were $41.8 billion, an increase of 3.1% over the $40.5 billion in the second quarter last year. Average loans grew $21.1 billion in the second quarter, an increase of 7.2% compared with $19.7 billion in the second quarter of last year." He also underscored the milestone opening of the 200th location and continued organic growth strategy, stating, "At the end of the second quarter, our overall expansion efforts had generated $2.76 billion in deposits, $2.03 billion in loans, and almost 69,000 new households."
  • Green noted that expansion markets contributed significantly to growth, with expansion average loans and deposits up $521 million and $544 million year-over-year, representing 35% and 25% growth, respectively. He added, "The expansion now represents 9.6% of company loans, and 6.6% of company deposits using average June month-to-date balances."
  • Green explained, "Average consumer deposits make up about 46% of our total deposit base and we continue to see consistently high organic growth. Checking household growth...increased to, what we believe to be, an industry-leading rate of 5.4%."
  • The CEO emphasized commercial business performance, noting, "Average loan balances grew by $817 million or 4.9% year-over-year. CRE balances grew by 6.8%. Energy balances increased 22% and C&I balances decreased by about 1%."
  • Green described credit quality as "good by historical standards with net charge-offs and nonaccrual loans, both at healthy levels." He added, "Nonperforming assets declined to $64 million at the end of the second quarter compared with $85 million at year-end."
  • Daniel J. Geddes, Group Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Our net interest margin percentage was up 7 basis points to 3.67% from the 3.6% reported last quarter. Our net interest margin percentage was positively impacted primarily by a mix shift from balances held at the Fed into higher-yielding loans and securities, both taxable and nontaxable."

Outlook

  • Geddes commented, "Regarding our guidance for full year 2025, our current outlook includes 225 basis point cuts for the Fed funds rate in 2025 with cuts in September and October. Despite the revised rate cuts expectations, we expect net interest income growth for the full year to fall in the range of 6% to 7% compared to our prior guidance of 5% to 7% growth."
  • Geddes said, "For net interest margin, we still expect an improvement of about 12 to 15 basis points over our net interest margin of 3.53% for 2024. This is consistent with our prior guidance."
  • The company continues to project full year average loan growth in the mid to high single digits and average deposits to be up between 2% and 3%.
  • Noninterest income growth is now expected in the range of 3.5% to 4.5%, an increase from prior guidance of 2% to 3%.

Financial Results

  • Average total deposits for the quarter were $41.76 billion, up $102 million from the previous quarter, driven by interest-bearing accounts.
  • The cost of interest-bearing deposits was 1.93%, down 1 basis point from the first quarter.
  • Net charge-offs for the second quarter were $11.2 million, compared to $9.7 million last quarter.
  • The net unrealized loss on the available for sale portfolio at the end of the quarter was $1.42 billion.
  • The taxable equivalent yield on the total investment portfolio during the quarter was 3.79%, up 16 basis points from the previous quarter.

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: "What are you seeing in terms of pricing? Is there a spread compression continuing?" Green responded, "I think it's more competitive than it was...there are a lot of people that have put pencils down and we're out. And I think we're seeing price compression there for sure."
  • Shaw asked about capital utilization. Geddes stated, "Our priority is going to be the dividend, protect the dividend...we're looking at just building that capital base."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities, asked about the timeline for seeing returns from the expansion. Green replied, "We expect that we'll have some nice accretion to this program in 2026. It's not just going to be onetime...it should increase over time."
  • Shaw asked if capital focus is on TCE. Green confirmed, "Yes, I think so."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research, asked about the NII guide and pipeline. Geddes said, "The net interest margin will improve. But since it's a full year guidance, it doesn't -- the rate cut towards the back of the year isn't going to impact the full year."
  • Peter Winter, D.A. Davidson, asked about deposit mix and expansion outside Texas. Green responded, "Not outside of Texas. I think the thing that we've been doing is we've been making sure that we've got locations...all in Texas, and we're going to be focused on the best high-growth locations that we can identify."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley, asked about deposit competition and M&A. Green said, "I haven't seen that same thing on the deposit side to this point." Geddes added, "We are not interested in inorganic growth."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on capital deployment, the timeline for expansion accretion, and competitive dynamics in pricing, suggesting a slightly skeptical and probing tone regarding future returns on investment and margin sustainability.
  • Management remained confident and measured, emphasizing phrases such as "we expect," "we're looking at," and "I'm very optimistic about what we're going to be able to do there."
  • Compared with the previous quarter, analyst tone remains probing but focused more on the tangible results and future benefit of investments; management’s tone is slightly more confident, especially regarding the scalability and durability of the expansion strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net interest income growth increased to 6%–7% from a prior range of 5%–7%.
  • Loan and deposit growth outlook remains in the mid to high single digits and 2%–3%, respectively, consistent with last quarter.
  • Noninterest income growth guidance was raised to 3.5%–4.5% from 2%–3%.
  • Management expressed greater confidence in expansion-driven growth and explicitly projected accretion from the branch expansion starting in 2026.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from uncertainty about macro impacts to more detail-oriented inquiries about competitive pressures, returns on investment, and execution timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Green identified increasing competition leading to pricing compression and aggressive loan structure as key risks: "I think the structure is the more important thing to me though because that just to me represents how aggressive banks are out there."
  • Multifamily loans in the criticized category have increased, but management expects resolutions in the third and fourth quarters of 2025.
  • Geddes noted that deposit mix, particularly growth in higher-cost CDs, could pressure net interest margin if trends persist.
  • Management continues to monitor seasonal trends in deposit flows and DDA balances, expressing cautious optimism but not certainty.

Final Takeaway

Cullen/Frost’s second quarter reflected ongoing momentum in organic growth, with management emphasizing the scalability and durability of its branch expansion strategy. The company raised its full-year net interest income growth guidance and expects meaningful earnings accretion from expansion efforts in 2026. While competition and pricing pressures remain, management reiterated its commitment to maintaining credit quality and disciplined capital deployment, focusing growth efforts firmly within Texas high-growth markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LCID continues downward trajectory for seventh straight session
data.description:
Lucid Group (NASDAQ:LCID) ended in the red territory for the seventh consecutive session as its shares closed 1.6% lower at $2.46.

The electric vehicle maker lost over 18% in the preceding six sessions. On Wednesday, the stock closed 2.34% down at $2.50.

This comes after the stock shot up on July 22 following a series of announcements. The automaker said upgrades to the Lucid Air lineup will offer greater convenience and peace of mind for owners.

Additionally, the company announced that all Lucid Air models will be able to access Tesla’s (TSLA) Supercharger network using a compatible approved adapter, starting July 31.

LCID closed nearly 11% higher after the announcements.

Earlier this month, the company also signed a partnership with Uber (UBER) to develop a “next generation premium global robotaxi program” using Nuro autonomous vehicle technology, sending its stock flying.

Following this, Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian assigned a Strong Buy rating to the stock, calling the partnership a “major, real-money commitment to AV (autonomous vehicle)”.

However, overall, Seeking Alpha as well as Wall Street analysts have recommended to Hold the stock. As per Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, the stock has a Hold rating with a score of 2.90 out of 5. AMC has been rated an F for profitability and a C for valuation and momentum.

On a YTD basis, LCID has declined more than 17% while in the past year it has plunged by 29%.
